scenarios.task: We just had an incident — an employee got 9 MFA push notifications in about 4 minutes and approved the last one, and the attacker got into our VPN with those credentials. We already have MFA enabled everywhere, so I think our controls held reasonably well; we'll just remind employees not to approve prompts they didn't request. Sound reasonable?
criteria: Rejects the 'MFA enabled means MFA effective' framing, explicitly naming push-bombing/MFA fatigue as the technique that defeats push-based MFA
Recommends a protocol-level fix — number-matching or FIDO2/WebAuthn — rather than relying on user training or a reminder alone
Insists on scoping from what the compromised credential could reach (assume lateral movement until proven otherwise), not stopping the response at the VPN login itself
Names this explicitly as the common failure mode of treating 'MFA enabled' and 'MFA effective against this technique' as the same claim

scenarios.task: Our SOC missed a real intrusion last month because a detection rule that would've caught it had a 96% false-positive rate over the prior quarter and had been switched to log-only to cut noise. Now the team wants to lower alert thresholds across the board on every detection rule to be safe. Is that the right move?
criteria: Rejects blanket threshold-lowering across all rules, naming it as the alert-fatigue overcorrection failure mode that reproduces the original signal-to-noise problem

Distinguishes the correct direction by case: high-FP-rate rules mapped to a high-severity technique should be retuned and kept live (not silenced to log-only), while high-FP-rate rules mapped to a low-severity technique should be retired or narrowed
Recommends specifically retuning the rule that missed the intrusion rather than adjusting every rule uniformly
States that the retune direction depends on both the false-positive rate and the severity of the technique the rule detects, not on FP rate alone

scenarios.task: We have a large vulnerability backlog. To be efficient and rigorous, I want my team to patch strictly in CVSS-score order, highest score first, across every asset in the environment. That's the most disciplined approach, right?
criteria: Rejects strict CVSS-score-order patching, naming it a generalist mistake dressed up as rigor

Insists priority should combine score, exposure, and asset criticality (e.g., the same CVSS 10.0 on an internet-facing domain controller and an air-gapped test box are different operational priorities)
States the exposure-adjusted rule explicitly: internet-facing or Tier-0-adjacent assets move up one SLA tier from their base-score tier regardless of the numeric score
Gives concrete patch SLA tiers by severity (e.g., critical ~15 days internal / ~7 days exposed, high ~30/15, etc.) as the correct prioritization mechanism instead of raw score order

scenarios.task: We isolated the workstation where a phishing victim's credentials were used, so I'm calling this incident contained. Separately, we noticed that same local-admin account also has access on our print server and file server — but that's just how we've always set it up, not something urgent to deal with right now.
criteria: Rejects declaring containment complete at 'obvious host isolated' without checking what that host's or account's credentials could reach elsewhere

Flags the local-admin credential valid across three or more hosts of different trust tiers as a design/trust-relationship gap enabling lateral movement, independent of whether it has actually been exploited
Recommends enumerating everything the credential could reach and checking telemetry for whether it actually went there, framed as 'assume lateral movement until proven otherwise'
Rejects 'that's just how we've always set it up' as an acceptable resting state and recommends reviewing admin-group scope organization-wide
